Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd get distracted or just not feel like it. Most of my 29 hours of studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep because of anxiety. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making colorful diagrams and mind maps, but the actual scheduling part was chaotic.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and panic-study. The regularity just wasn't there for me this semester. Between my extracurriculars and just feeling unmotivated most days, consistent timing felt impossible.
Q3: Usually it was panic, to be honest. I'd get an email reminder about something due, or I'd suddenly remember at 2 AM that I hadn't checked the course in a week. Sometimes a classmate would mention something in our group chat and I'd realize I was behind. It was rarely a proactive choice to log in.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a habit for me. I logged in maybe 17 times the whole course, which tells you something. I tried setting reminders on my phone at one point, but I'd just dismiss them. It always felt like a chore rather than something natural.
Q5: The video content was somewhat helpful because I learn visually. I'd watch them when I actually logged in, maybe about 20 minutes worth on average. The diagrams and visual explanations stuck with me better than reading walls of text. But even then, I wouldn't say I used them to their full potential.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums. I posted maybe 14 times total, but a lot of those were just to meet requirements. The readings I'd often skip or skim really quickly. I told myself I'd come back to them, but rarely did. They just felt overwhelming when I was already anxious about everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment. Anything that felt abstract or like busywork, I'd push aside. If I could visualize the outcome or see how it helped my grade, I was more likely to engage.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I didn't do it. I was already struggling to keep up with mandatory stuff, so adding extra work felt impossible. Required activities at least had the pressure of consequences, which sometimes was the only thing that got me moving.
Q9: Yes, a lot actually. I'd download PDFs and diagrams, then study from my tablet offline. I also used YouTube videos and some external websites that explained concepts visually. My notes were mostly handwritten with lots of colors and drawings. The LMS wouldn't show any of that time.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my desk for hours with my downloaded materials and handwritten notes, but I wasn't logged into anything. I'd be drawing concept maps, rewatching YouTube explanations, or going through flashcards I made. None of that showed up anywhere online.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, then never go back to the LMS for it. I preferred having everything saved locally so I didn't need internet or to deal with logging in. It was just easier to study from my own organized folders.
Q12: There were definitely hard moments, especially mid-semester when my anxiety peaked. I'd feel paralyzed looking at all the work piling up. My attendance was basically nonexistent because I couldn't bring myself to show up when I felt so behind. The stress made everything feel impossible.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd just avoid the LMS entirely. It became associated with stress for me. My extracurriculars also took up mental energy, and sometimes I'd prioritize those because they actually made me feel good, unlike coursework which just made me anxious.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, scroll through materials, maybe watch a few minutes of video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was physically present but mentally checked out. The anxiety made it hard to actually absorb anything even when I was trying.
Q15: I postponed things constantly. The bigger and more important something felt, the more I avoided it. It's like my brain couldn't handle the pressure. I'd do smaller, less important tasks instead, or just distract myself entirely. I knew it was self-sabotage but couldn't stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then scramble to submit. My 97% assignment completion rate happened through pure panic-driven effort. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually received it, but I often avoided checking it because I was scared of criticism. When feedback was positive, it gave me a small boost. But mostly I'd submit things and then not want to know how I did because of the anxiety.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming to me. There was so much content and I never felt like I knew where to start. Having seven online courses at once made it worse. I needed more guidance on prioritizing, but instead I just felt lost in all the options.
Q19: The visual materials helped me engage more easily. But the sheer volume of content and the way things were organized made it harder. I wished there were clearer pathways or checklists. The open-ended nature of it triggered my anxiety about doing things wrong.
Q20: I think some students might be like me, where they're logging in and clicking around but not actually absorbing anything. You can spend hours in the LMS feeling paralyzed, reading the same paragraph repeatedly. Activity doesn't equal productivity, especially when you're anxious.
Q21: That's pretty much my situation. My exam score was decent and I passed, but my LMS activity was minimal. I did most of my real learning offline with downloaded materials and external resources. The LMS just doesn't capture studying that happens away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked things and how long pages were open. But they miss whether you actually understood anything, whether you were anxious the whole time, or if you studied extensively offline. They're just tracking surface behaviors, not real learning.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and want to learn more. I wasn't really engaged this semester, even when I was completing assignments. I was just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety attacks before logging in, or how the mindfulness exercises actually helped me feel better by the end. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with offline materials, or how my extracurriculars kept me sane. It just sees clicks, not the whole person.
